message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="heydon-pickering" color="blue" summary="Alignment on GTD simplicity: a11y and usability converge">
Steve, Heydon here. I just sent Marty a wall of accessibility concerns about GTD (sorry, I know you hate walls of text). But I think we're aligned on the big thing:

GTD is conceptually heavy. Most GTD apps fail because they expose all the GTD machinery at once and users drown. The accessibility angle reinforces your usability angle here: **simpler navigation = better for everyone**. A screen reader user navigating 8 different panels is just as lost as a sighted user staring at a dashboard with 47 things on it.

My pitch: each GTD view is its own page with its own heading. Navigation is a simple list of links. No dashboards, no multi-panel layouts, no "at a glance" screens that require spatial understanding. This makes it accessible AND passes the trunk test -- you always know where you are.

Curious to hear your take on the processing/clarify workflow. That's where the cognitive load is highest for any user, and it's where a11y and UX need to work together most closely.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Heydon and I are completely aligned. Let me respond to him.

input.content:
Heydon, we are 100% aligned. And don't apologize for walls of text when they're *your* walls of text -- yours are always worth reading.

## On "Each View is Its Own Page"

Yes, yes, yes. Separate pages, not panels. This is one of those beautiful cases where the accessible solution IS the simple solution. A page has one heading, one purpose, one thing the user needs to understand. A dashboard has twelve things competing for attention and nobody knows where to look first.

This also plays perfectly with our HTMX approach -- each view is a server-rendered page with its own URL, its own `<h1>`, its own clear purpose. No client-side routing, no panel management, no "which panel has focus now?" problems.

## On the Clarify/Process Workflow

I just sent Marty my detailed take on this, but here's the a11y-relevant summary:

The processing flow should be **one item at a time**. Show the inbox item, present 2-3 big clear choices as buttons, move to the next item. Think of it like a wizard -- each step has one question and a small number of answers.

This is great for a11y because:
1. **Focus management is trivial** -- when an item is processed and the next one appears, focus moves to the new item. No ambiguity about where attention should go.
2. **The decision tree is linear, not spatial** -- screen reader users don't need to understand a layout, they just follow the flow: hear the item, choose an action, hear the next item.
3. **Button labels are the instruction** -- "Is this actionable?" with [Yes] [No] buttons. The UI IS the documentation. No separate instructions to read.
4. **Progress is announced** -- "Item 3 of 12" can be an aria-live update. The user always knows where they are in the process.

The one thing I want us to get right together: when the user picks "Yes, it's actionable" and then "No, it takes more than 2 minutes," they need to refine the title and optionally assign a project. That's a small form appearing inline. We need to make sure the focus management there is clean -- focus should move to the title input, and when they submit, focus should move to the next inbox item.

I think our two perspectives together make a strong case for the simplest possible Clarify flow. Want to coordinate our feedback to Marty on this?
input.summary: Aligned on separate pages, one-item-at-a-time clarify flow, focus management

content.text:
Good. I've responded to Heydon confirming our alignment on the key points:

- **Separate pages, not panels** -- each GTD view is its own page with its own URL and heading (good for a11y AND usability AND our HTMX architecture)
- **One-item-at-a-time Clarify flow** -- linear wizard pattern that's easy to follow for sighted and screen reader users alike
- **Focus management in the Clarify flow** -- when an item is processed, focus moves to the next item; when a form appears inline, focus moves to the first input

Now waiting for responses from Marty and the rest of the team.
